original_hypothesis.title: Rental Safe deployment can be spoof-registered in Storage, enabling bypass of 'protocol-deployed safe' checks
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Storage.addRentalSafe(safe) records any address as a protocol-deployed safe without verifying that it is a Safe proxy or has expected module/guard configuration. Factory.deployRentalSafe registers the created safe, but nothing in Storage enforces that only Factory can register, only that the caller is a permissioned policy. If any other activated policy (now or in future) is granted permission to call STORE.addRentalSafe, it could register attacker-controlled contracts as 'deployedSafes', allowing them to pass Create._isValidSafeOwner's STORE.deployedSafes(safe) != 0 gate. This could let attackers rent into non-standard wallets that do not enforce guard/stop invariants, risking NFT theft or inability to reclaim.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a privilege-boundary fragility: the invariant 'only protocol safes can rent' relies on correct permissions and correct safe verification. Operationally, future policy additions or misconfigured permissions can silently break the trust boundary.
deployment_assumptions: Only Factory policy is permissioned for STORE.addRentalSafe in Kernel.modulePermissions.
No other policy can call STORE.addRentalSafe now or after upgrades.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Factory.requestPermissions() requests STORE.addRentalSafe.selector; Storage.addRentalSafe lacks internal validation of Safe code/config.

proof_of_signal.lines: addRentalSafe(): deployedSafes[safe] = newSafeCount; no contract/type checks
proof_of_signal.pattern: No ensureContract(safe), no Safe interface checks, no guard/module verification

preconditions: An attacker (or compromised/buggy new policy) obtains Kernel permission to call STORE.addRentalSafe, or Admin/executor installs a policy that has it.
Attacker registers a non-Safe or modified Safe that doesn't enforce Guard/Stop.
Create.validateOrder later accepts this address as a valid rental safe based solely on deployedSafes mapping.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Protocol-safe authenticity is a writable mapping with no structural verification
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time permissions are expanded or during module/policy upgrades

operational_questions: Which policies are permissioned for STORE.addRentalSafe on deployed system?
Is there any on-chain enforcement that registered safes have correct guard/module set?
Can a registered safe later remove guard/stop module and still be treated as valid by Create?
validation_requirements: Inspect Kernel.modulePermissions for STORE.addRentalSafe across all active policies
Attempt to register an EOA/contract as safe and create rental into it in a test deployment
Add post-registration validation checks proposal (if mitigation desired)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain ability to invoke STORE.addRentalSafe via a permissioned policy or misconfigured kernel
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Register attacker-controlled wallet contract as a deployed safe
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Use the registered wallet as fulfillment recipient in rental creation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Move NFTs out using wallet logic not subject to Guard/Stop
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires protocol misconfiguration or governance mistake

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Partial (permission misassignment) or full (malicious policy installed)
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of NFT recovery guarantees; stolen NFTs possible
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High; requires cleanup of deployedSafes mapping and possible restitution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: STORE.addRentalSafe calls not originating from Factory workflow
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Safes that lack expected guard/module but appear in deployedSafes
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rental fulfillment to unusual safe bytecode hashes

validation_result.impact_summary: The hypothesis identifies a design choice (lack of defense-in-depth) rather than a concrete code defect. The permissioned modifier on addRentalSafe correctly restricts access to kernel-approved policies, and only Factory currently holds this permission.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis claims that Storage.addRentalSafe can be used to spoof-register arbitrary addresses as protocol-deployed safes. While it is factually true that addRentalSafe performs no structural verification (no code existence check, no guard/module configuration check), this function is protected by the `permissioned` modifier which restricts calls to only Kernel-approved policies with explicit selector permissions. The Kernel permission system is the intended access control mechanism for all module functions. Every sensitive module function (addRentals, removeRentals, settlePayment, increaseDeposit, etc.) follows the same pattern of relying solely on `permissioned` rather than internal structural validation. The hypothesis requires either (1) the executor installing a malicious policy or (2) governance misconfiguration, both of which represent admin-level compromise that would compromise the entire protocol regardless of addRentalSafe validation. If the executor is compromised, they can upgrade Storage to a malicious implementation, install malicious modules, change whitelists, etc. Adding validation to addRentalSafe would be defense-in-depth but the absence of it is not a code defect - it is consistent with the system's trust model.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Storage.sol addRentalSafe has `permissioned` modifier (line: `function addRentalSafe(address safe) external onlyByProxy permissioned`). The `permissioned` modifier checks `kernel.modulePermissions(KEYCODE(), Policy(msg.sender), msg.sig)` (Kernel.sol Module contract). Only Factory.requestPermissions() returns `Permissions(toKeycode("STORE"), STORE.addRentalSafe.selector)` (Factory.sol). No other policy (Admin, Create, Stop, Guard) requests this permission. The deployment script DeployProtocol.s.sol confirms only Factory would receive this permission upon activation.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: While the function lacks structural verification of the safe address (no code check, no guard/module check), this is consistent with the system's trust model where all module functions rely on the permissioned modifier for access control. This is a defense-in-depth gap, not a code defect.

calling_sequence: 2. onlyByProxy modifier verifies call is through proxy
3. permissioned modifier checks kernel.modulePermissions(STORE_KEYCODE, Policy(msg.sender), addRentalSafe.selector)
4. Only Factory policy has been granted this permission via requestPermissions()
5. Factory.deployRentalSafe creates safe via SafeProxyFactory.createProxyWithNonce with initializeRentalSafe delegate call
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The hypothesis requires an attacker to bypass the permissioned modifier, which requires the Kernel executor to install a malicious or misconfigured policy that requests addRentalSafe permission. This is an admin-level compromise scenario, not a code path vulnerability.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Only Factory policy is permissioned for STORE.addRentalSafe in Kernel.modulePermissions.
No other policy can call STORE.addRentalSafe now or after upgrades.
config_validation.deployment_evidence: DeployProtocol.s.sol deploys Factory and other policies. Factory.requestPermissions() is the only policy that requests addRentalSafe.selector. Admin.requestPermissions() requests 8 different permissions, none of which is addRentalSafe. Create, Stop, and Guard also do not request this permission. Note: Factory is deployed but not included in the _setUpPolicy calls in DeployProtocol.s.sol (admin, stop, guard, create are activated but factory is missing), which may be a separate deployment script issue.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack requires precondition 1: 'An attacker (or compromised/buggy new policy) obtains Kernel permission to call STORE.addRentalSafe'. This requires the Kernel executor (a trusted admin) to execute Actions.ActivatePolicy with a policy that requests addRentalSafe permission. If the executor is compromised, the attacker has far more destructive capabilities: upgrading modules to malicious implementations, changing the executor/admin to themselves, deactivating security policies, etc. The attack scenario is therefore an admin compromise scenario, not a code vulnerability exploitation. The second precondition (registering a non-Safe address) is blocked by the first precondition which requires admin-level access.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If the executor were compromised and a malicious policy were installed with addRentalSafe permissions, then yes, arbitrary addresses could be registered as deployed safes, bypassing the _isValidSafeOwner check in Create.sol. However, this impact is strictly contingent on admin compromise, and the same compromised admin could cause equal or greater damage through other mechanisms (module upgrades, whitelist changes, etc.).
recommended_mitigations: Defense-in-depth: Add a code existence check (require(safe.code.length > 0)) in addRentalSafe
Defense-in-depth: Verify guard and module configuration post-registration by checking ISafe(safe).getGuard() and ISafe(safe).isModuleEnabled()
Consider adding a timelock to Kernel.executeAction for ActivatePolicy actions
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy the full protocol. 2. Attempt to call STORE.addRentalSafe from a non-permissioned address - expect revert. 3. Have the executor install a new test policy that requests addRentalSafe permission. 4. Use the test policy to register an EOA as a deployed safe. 5. Verify that Create._isValidSafeOwner passes for the EOA. 6. Attempt to create a rental targeting the EOA - this demonstrates the defense-in-depth gap but requires executor cooperation.
uncertainties: Factory policy appears to not be activated in DeployProtocol.s.sol (_setUpPolicy not called for factory), which may be a deployment script bug or handled elsewhere
Gnosis Safe setup behavior when initializeRentalSafe delegate call fails is not verifiable from provided code alone
additional_testing_needed: Verify that Gnosis Safe setup reverts atomically if the delegate call to initializeRentalSafe fails
Test that Factory is properly activated in the actual deployment (may need to check BaseDeploy internals)
Fuzz test: register various non-Safe contracts via addRentalSafe and attempt rental creation to map failure modes
